cards.front: What are the three zones of the adrenal cortex and what hormone does each produce?
cards.back: Zona glomerulosa produces mineralocorticoids (aldosterone and deoxycorticosterone); zona fasciculata produces glucocorticoids (cortisol/hydrocortisone and corticosterone); zona reticularis produces androgens (DHEA), estrogens, and small amounts of progesterone.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What are the main catecholamines produced by the adrenal medulla?
cards.back: Epinephrine (adrenaline) and norepinephrine (noradrenaline).
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What is Addison's disease and is it primary or secondary adrenal insufficiency?
cards.back: Addison's disease is primary adrenal insufficiency where there is destruction of the adrenal gland itself, resulting in compensatory increased ACTH levels as the pituitary attempts to stimulate the damaged glands.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: Name the main causes of primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison's disease).
cards.back: Autoimmune atrophy, infections (tuberculosis, fungal, viral), infiltrations (amyloidosis, hemochromatosis), hemorrhage (meningococcal septicemia), anticoagulants, drugs (ketoconazole, metyrapone), enzyme inducers (rifampicin, phenytoin), cytotoxic agents (mitotane), and adrenalectomy.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What is the difference between primary and secondary adrenal insufficiency in terms of ACTH levels?
cards.back: In primary insufficiency (Addison's), ACTH is elevated as the pituitary tries to compensate; in secondary insufficiency (pituitary problem), ACTH is low because the pituitary cannot produce it.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What are the clinical features of Addison's disease?
cards.back: Insidious onset of weight loss, malaise, anorexia, vomiting, dehydration, hyperpigmentation (over bony prominences, exposed areas, palmar creases, scars, nails), sparse axillary and pubic hair, postural hypotension, amenorrhea, infertility, hypoglycemia, susceptibility to infections, and psychiatric problems. In severe cases, Addisonian crisis with shock and coma.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: Why does hyperpigmentation occur specifically in primary adrenal insufficiency?
cards.back: Because as ACTH increases to compensate for low cortisol, ACTH stimulates melanocytes via its melanocyte-stimulating hormone (MSH) activity. This does not occur in secondary insufficiency where ACTH levels are low.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What is an Addisonian crisis and what are the precipitating factors?
cards.back: A life-threatening acute exacerbation of adrenal insufficiency characterized by severe hypotension, tachycardia, vomiting, abdominal pain, and potentially coma. Precipitating factors include infection, stress, and drugs.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What are the key findings on basic investigations in a patient with Addison's disease?
cards.back: Urea and electrolytes: low sodium, high potassium, increased urea (prerenal dysfunction). Full blood count: anemia with eosinophilia and elevated ESR. Hypoglycemia and hypocalcemia may be present.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What is the definitive test to confirm adrenal insufficiency and how is it performed?
cards.back: Serum cortisol measured in the morning (08:00-09:00) when cortisol is expected to be highest. If cortisol is low at this peak time, adrenal insufficiency is confirmed. ACTH level is then measured to distinguish primary from secondary.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: Describe the short synacthen (ACTH) stimulation test.
cards.back: A 1 microgram dose of intravenous ACTH is given, with cortisol measured at baseline, 30 minutes, and 60 minutes. In normal individuals, cortisol rises above 550 nanomoles per liter. Failure to rise indicates adrenal insufficiency.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: How does the long synacthen stimulation test differentiate between primary and secondary adrenal insufficiency?
cards.back: With primary disease (destroyed adrenal), repeated ACTH stimulation produces no cortisol response over 4 days. With secondary disease (pituitary problem), continuous ACTH stimulation gradually kickstarts the gland, causing cortisol to rise by day 4 (>550 nanomoles per liter).
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What is the maintenance treatment for primary adrenal insufficiency?
cards.back: Hydrocortisone 10mg in morning, 5mg at lunch and evening (or prednisone 3mg morning, 1.25mg evening as alternative). Plus mineralocorticoid replacement: fludrocortisone 0.05-0.1mg daily.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What is the treatment of an Addisonian crisis?
cards.back: Intravenous fluids (normal saline or dextrose saline) and high-dose intravenous hydrocortisone 100mg stat, then 6-hourly for 24-48 hours. Empiric antibiotics for presumed infection if there is no clear history of non-compliance.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What is Cushing's syndrome and what is the main clinical feature that helps identify it?
cards.back: Cushing's syndrome is excessive glucocorticoid secretion from the adrenal cortex. The most characteristic clinical finding is truncal obesity with thin arms and legs (disproportionate obesity or lemon on a matchstick appearance).
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What is the difference between Cushing's syndrome and Cushing's disease?
cards.back: Cushing's syndrome is any cause of hypercortisolism. Cushing's disease specifically refers to secondary hypercortisolism caused by a pituitary adenoma producing excess ACTH.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: Name the main types of Cushing's syndrome based on etiology.
cards.back: Primary (20-25%): bilateral adrenal hyperplasia or unilateral adenoma/carcinoma. Secondary (75%): pituitary adenoma (Cushing's disease) or increased CRH from hypothalamus. Ectopic: ACTH or CRH from non-pituitary tumors (bronchial carcinoma, carcinoid, pancreatic carcinoma, ovarian tumors). Drugs: exogenous glucocorticoids. Pseudo-Cushing's: alcohol or depression.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What are the facial and trunk features of Cushing's syndrome?
cards.back: Moon facies with plethora and telangiectasia, exophthalmos (fat deposits), hirsutism (upper lip and jaw), acne on face/chest/shoulders, and truncal obesity with thin limbs. Buffalo hump (interscapular fat), and purple stretch marks (striae) more than 1cm wide on flanks, hips, abdomen, and thighs.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What is proximal myopathy in Cushing's syndrome caused by and what is its significance?
cards.back: It is caused by excessive protein catabolism due to glucocorticoid excess. Clinically, patients develop wasting of proximal muscles (shoulders, hips) which contrasts with the truncal obesity.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What metabolic complications of Cushing's syndrome are commonly seen?
cards.back: Secondary hypertension (due to mineralocorticoid effects), dysglycemia (glucose intolerance, pre-diabetes, or diabetes), hypokalemic alkalosis, hypercalciuria, and hyperlipidemia.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What psychiatric and neurological manifestations can occur in Cushing's syndrome?
cards.back: Neurosis, psychosis, depression, paranoid psychosis. Benign intracranial hypertension causing papilledema. The difficulty is that depression is both a cause (pseudo-Cushing's from depression) and an effect of Cushing's syndrome.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What is the primary biochemical test to diagnose hypercortisolism?
cards.back: 24-hour urine free cortisol, which is elevated. Other tests include midnight serum cortisol or salivary cortisol (both elevated due to loss of normal diurnal variation).
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: Describe the low-dose dexamethasone suppression test (1mg overnight DST).
cards.back: Dexamethasone 1mg is given at 11pm, and cortisol is measured between 8-9am the next day. In normal individuals, dexamethasone suppresses cortisol. In Cushing's syndrome, cortisol is NOT suppressed (remains elevated).
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What is the standard low-dose dexamethasone suppression test protocol?
cards.back: 0.5mg dexamethasone given 6-hourly for 48 hours, with serum cortisol measured on day 3. In normal individuals, cortisol falls. In Cushing's syndrome, cortisol remains elevated.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: How is serum ACTH measured used to determine the etiology of Cushing's syndrome?
cards.back: Low ACTH indicates primary (adrenal) Cushing's. High ACTH indicates either secondary (pituitary) or ectopic ACTH secretion, requiring further testing to distinguish.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: Describe the high-dose dexamethasone suppression test and how it distinguishes pituitary from ectopic ACTH secretion.
cards.back: 2mg dexamethasone 6-hourly for 48 hours. In pituitary Cushing's, there is suppression of urine free cortisol (>90% by day 3). In ectopic ACTH, there is NO suppression. In primary adrenal, ACTH is low so the distinction doesn't apply.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What other diagnostic tests are used for Cushing's syndrome when further differentiation is needed?
cards.back: CRF/DDAVP test to assess ACTH and cortisol response. Inferior petrosal sinus sampling (IPSS) with and without corticotropin-releasing factor to localize a pituitary adenoma. Imaging with CT or MRI to identify adrenal or pituitary tumors.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What are the ancillary investigations in Cushing's syndrome?
cards.back: Urea and electrolytes: hypokalemic alkalosis. Full blood count: increased hemoglobin/RBC (polycythemia), decreased WBC (eosinopenia), low ESR. Chest X-ray for hypertension or mediastinal pathology (if ectopic source suspected). ECG for hypertension. Bone mineral density (DXA) for osteoporosis screening.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What is the diagnostic approach using the overnight low-dose dexamethasone suppression test?
cards.back: Give 1 mg dexamethasone at night and measure serum cortisol the next morning. Normal suppression is >50% of baseline cortisol.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: How does the high-dose overnight dexamethasone test work?
cards.back: Give 8 mg dexamethasone at night and measure serum cortisol the next day. >68% suppression indicates pituitary cause; <68% suggests ectopic ACTH.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What is the purpose of the ACTH and cortisol response to CRF or DDAVP?
cards.back: Distinguish pituitary from ectopic ACTH. Pituitary: 50% increase in ACTH, 20% increase in cortisol. Ectopic: minimal or no response.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What is inferior petrosal sinus sampling and when is it used?
cards.back: Invasive test to distinguish pituitary from ectopic ACTH. Measures central (petrosal sinus) vs peripheral ACTH. Marked gradient favors pituitary; no difference suggests ectopic.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: How does the Metyrapone test help in Cushing's syndrome diagnosis?
cards.back: Blocks 11-beta-hydroxylase. Pituitary cause: increased precursors and cortisol precursors. Ectopic: no reduction in cortisol or precursor increase.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What are the treatment options for pituitary Cushing's syndrome?
cards.back: Surgery, radiotherapy, bromocriptine, or cabergoline. If these fail, bilateral adrenalectomy may be needed but carries risk of secondary adrenal insufficiency.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: How is adrenal Cushing's syndrome managed?
cards.back: If adenoma: surgery. If carcinoma: surgery (if resectable), radiotherapy, mitotane, OPDDD, or enzyme blockers like aminoglutethimide.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What is medical adrenalectomy and when is it used?
cards.back: Ketoconazole, used for ectopic ACTH Cushing's when the tumor cannot be located or is aggressive. Blocks cortisol synthesis.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What is mifepristone and its role in Cushing's syndrome?
cards.back: Glucocorticoid receptor blocker used for severe cortisol-induced symptoms. Not widely available.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What is Nelson syndrome and how does it develop?
cards.back: Enlargement of pituitary tumor with increased ACTH after bilateral adrenalectomy. Occurs when pituitary is the primary cause but was masked by high cortisol, causing hyperpigmentation and visual/cranial nerve symptoms.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: How is Nelson syndrome treated?
cards.back: Surgery to remove the enlarged pituitary tumor or dopamine agonists (bromocriptine, analogs, pasireotide).
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What is the prognosis of untreated Cushing's syndrome?
cards.back: Fatal within 5 years if untreated. Death results from cardiovascular disease, diabetes, hypertension complications, or infection.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What are the cure rates for treated Cushing's syndrome by cause?
cards.back: Primary adrenal adenoma: 100% cure rate. Primary adrenal carcinoma: poor prognosis. Pituitary (Cushing's disease): 95% cure rate.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What indicates successful surgical treatment of Cushing's syndrome?
cards.back: Serum cortisol becomes normal or low within 24 hours of successful intervention.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What is primary hyperaldosteroneism?
cards.back: Autonomous hypersecretion of aldosterone by the adrenal gland (zona glomerulosa) causing hypertension, hypokalemia, and metabolic alkalosis.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What are the major causes of primary hyperaldosteroneism?
cards.back: Aldosterone-producing adenoma (Conn's syndrome): 99% of cases, usually unilateral. Idiopathic hyperaldosteroneism (bilateral adrenal hyperplasia). Rarely carcinoma or glucocorticoid-remediable hyperaldosteroneism.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: Who is most commonly affected by primary hyperaldosteroneism?
cards.back: More common in women than men. Typically age 30-50 years.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What are the key clinical features of primary hyperaldosteroneism?
cards.back: Hypertension (mild to severe), hypokalemia effects (muscle weakness, myopathy, tetany), polyuria, polydipsia, NO edema (escape phenomenon), nocturia.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What is the significance of finding tetany with hypertension?
cards.back: Tetany + hypertension in untreated patient is characteristic of Conn's syndrome (primary hyperaldosteroneism), indicating severe hypokalemia.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: Why is there no edema in primary hyperaldosteroneism?
cards.back: Escape phenomenon occurs: initial sodium retention triggers volume expansion, which suppresses aldosterone, and natriuretic peptide is released to prevent edema.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What renal effects occur from mineralocorticoid excess?
cards.back: Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus (inability to concentrate urine causing polyuria/polydipsia), vacuolar nephropathy, recurrent pyelonephritis, and possible kidney damage from hypertension.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What are the basal biochemical tests for primary hyperaldosteroneism?
cards.back: Urine electrolytes (show hypokalemic alkalosis), plasma renin (decreased), plasma aldosterone (increased, measured as concentration or activity).
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What provocative tests establish aldosterone non-suppressibility?
cards.back: Saline infusion test or captopril test to confirm that aldosterone cannot be suppressed despite attempted suppression.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What radiological investigations are used?
cards.back: CT of adrenal glands to determine if unilateral (adenoma) or bilateral (hyperplasia). Adrenal vein sampling may also be used.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What aldosterone-to-renin ratio indicates primary hyperaldosteroneism?
cards.back: If measuring plasma renin activity: ratio >50 with aldosterone >555 pmol/L. If measuring renin concentration: ratio >90.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: How is primary hyperaldosteroneism managed?
cards.back: Adenoma: surgical removal. Idiopathic hyperaldosteroneism (bilateral hyperplasia): poorly responsive to surgery; use mineralocorticoid antagonists (spironolactone non-selective or eplerenone selective).
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: How do you differentiate primary from secondary hyperaldosteroneism?
cards.back: Primary: low renin + high aldosterone + non-suppressible. Secondary (renal disease, liver, cardiac failure): both renin and aldosterone increased. Other causes: low renin + low aldosterone.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What is pheochromocytoma?
cards.back: Benign or malignant tumor of chromaffin cells (adrenal medulla = pheochromocytoma; extra-adrenal sympathetic ganglia = paraganglioma) causing excessive catecholamine secretion.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What percentage of secondary hypertension is caused by pheochromocytoma?
cards.back: About 1% of secondary hypertension cases.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What is the difference between pheochromocytoma and paraganglioma?
cards.back: Pheochromocytoma: adrenal medulla origin. Paraganglioma: extra-adrenal chromaffin cell origin (sympathetic ganglia). Both can be called pheochromocytoma broadly.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What percentage of pheochromocytomas are sporadic vs familial?
cards.back: 95% sporadic, <5% familial. Familial ones are often bilateral and associated with MEN 2A or 2B syndromes.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What is the rule of 10s for pheochromocytoma?
cards.back: 10% extra-adrenal, 10% malignant, 10% bilateral (especially in children), 10% occur in children. Note: this rule doesn't hold universally.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What is the most common site of extra-adrenal pheochromocytoma?
cards.back: Organs of Zuckerkandl (celiac and superior mesenteric ganglia), which lie between the inferior and superior mesenteric arteries. Other sites: thorax, bladder (~1% each), neck (<1%).
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: Which genes are associated with familial pheochromocytoma?
cards.back: RET gene (MEN 2), NF1 gene (neurofibromatosis type 1), succinate dehydrogenase (SDH) mutations, and VHL gene.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What are the classic symptoms of pheochromocytoma?
cards.back: Headache, sweating, palpitations. Now called the 5 Ps: Pressure (hypertension), Pain (headache), Perspiration (sweating), Palpitations, Pallor.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: How is hypertension characterized in pheochromocytoma?
cards.back: 60% sustained, 40% paroxysmal. Often severe or malignant. Resistant to standard therapy (requires >5 agents). Half of sustained HTN patients also have paroxysms.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What precipitates paroxysmal crises in abdominal pheochromocytoma?
cards.back: Any factor displacing abdominal contents: palpation of abdomen, exertion, or physical manipulation. Crises last 10-15 minutes to several hours.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What symptoms occur during pheochromocytoma crises?
cards.back: Headache, sweating, palpitations, pallor, apprehension, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, tachycardia, angina, dyspnea. 1/3 have glycosuria only during attack; 1/3 have HTN only during attack.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: Why can pheochromocytoma cause postural hypotension?
cards.back: Occurs with predominantly epinephrine-secreting tumors. Due to decreased plasma volume and blunted sympathetic response.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What other complications can pheochromocytoma cause?
cards.back: Weight loss, hyperpigmentation, cardiac failure (from prolonged hypertension or catecholamine-induced cardiomyopathy), rhabdomyolysis from severe vasoconstriction causing muscle ischemia.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What hematological findings occur in pheochromocytoma?
cards.back: Increased hematocrit and erythrocytosis (from erythropoietin production), increased ESR and fever (from IL-6 production), hypercalcemia.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What urinary finding occurs with bladder pheochromocytoma?
cards.back: Hematuria (from bladder irritation). Paroxysms can be triggered by micturition.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What biochemical test is the gold standard for pheochromocytoma?
cards.back: Plasma free metanephrine (gold standard, most sensitive). Alternative: 24-hour urine metanephrine.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What other biochemical tests help diagnose pheochromocytoma?
cards.back: 24-hour urine catecholamines (epinephrine, norepinephrine, dopamine), serum chromogranin A (elevated).
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: Why is ensuring medication compliance important before pheochromocytoma testing?
cards.back: Dietary agents and drugs can interfere with catecholamine and metanephrine measurements, causing false positives.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What imaging studies localize pheochromocytoma?
cards.back: CT or MRI of abdomen (first-line for adrenal). MIBG scan and PET scan (fluorodopamine, dotatate) are increasingly used. Ultrasound, chest/abdominal X-ray, selective venous sampling, and aortography are less commonly used now.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: Why is MIBG scan useful for pheochromocytoma?
cards.back: Localizes tumors AND can be used therapeutically for malignant pheochromocytoma.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What is the most important principle of preoperative preparation for pheochromocytoma surgery?
cards.back: Avoid unopposed alpha or beta adrenergic activity. Give alpha-blockade FIRST, then beta-blockers if needed for tachycardia control.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What alpha-blockers are used for pheochromocytoma preparation?
cards.back: Phenoxybenzamine (non-selective), or selective agents: prazosin, doxazosin, terazosin.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: Why must beta-blockers never be started before alpha-blockade?
cards.back: Without alpha-blockade, beta-blockers allow unopposed alpha-adrenergic activity, causing disastrous hypertension.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What is alpha-methyltyrosine and when is it used?
cards.back: Rate-limiting enzyme inhibitor for catecholamine synthesis. Used for patients with malignant pheochromocytoma who cannot have surgery or refuse it.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What medical therapy is used for inoperable or malignant pheochromocytoma?
cards.back: Alpha-adrenergic blockade, alpha-methyltyrosine, and MIBG therapy (which can be therapeutic for malignancy). Avoid beta-blockers without prior alpha-blockade.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What is the 5-year survival rate for benign pheochromocytoma?
cards.back: >95% five-year survival. Recurrence rate <10%. Catecholamines return to normal within a week of surgery.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What is the 5-year survival rate for malignant pheochromocytoma?
cards.back: <50% five-year survival.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: When does hypertension persist after pheochromocytoma surgery?
cards.back: Either the pheochromocytoma wasn't completely removed, or the patient has concomitant essential hypertension.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What is the long-term follow-up strategy for pheochromocytoma?
cards.back: Lifelong follow-up. Assess catecholamine levels periodically for several years in asymptomatic patients, then continue lifetime surveillance.
cards.source: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015
cards.sourceFile: Adrenal Gland - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-015.yml

cards.front: What is diabetes mellitus in terms of metabolic state?
cards.back: Diabetes is a state of chronic hyperglycemia associated with metabolic problems other than glucose metabolism, due to either a defect or abnormality of insulin secretion and/or action.
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What is the mortality increase in type 1 and type 2 diabetes compared to general population?
cards.back: Type 1 diabetes: 4-7 fold increased mortality. Type 2 diabetes: 2-3 fold increased mortality.
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: Name the three main microvascular complications of diabetes.
cards.back:
1) Retinopathy (damage to blood vessels in the retina)
2) Nephropathy (kidney disease)
3) Neuropathy (nerve damage)
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What is the management objective for diabetes control?
cards.back: Achieve normal or near-normal glycemia with adequate control of blood pressure, lipids, and obesity using diet, exercise, oral anti-diabetic agents, insulin, and other newer injectables.
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What are the clinical stages of glycemia?
cards.back:
1) Normal glycemia (normal glucose regulation)
2) Hyperglycemia (impaired glucose tolerance or impaired glucose regulation/pre-diabetes)
3) Diabetes (not insulin requiring, insulin requiring for control, or insulin requiring for survival)
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What percentage of diabetes is type 1 vs type 2?
cards.back: Type 1: 5-10% of all diabetes. Type 2: Over 90% of all diabetes.
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What is the typical pathogenesis of type 2 diabetes?
cards.back: Starts with insulin resistance. Fasting hyperglycemia only becomes manifest when there is insulin secretory defect.
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: List the etiological types of diabetes recognized by 2019 WHO classification.
cards.back:
1) Type 1 (autoimmune or idiopathic)
2) Type 2
3) Other specific types
4) Hybrid form (LADA and ketosis-prone type 2)
5) Unclassified
6) Hyperglycemia during pregnancy
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What is LADA?
cards.back: LADA (Latent Autoimmune Diabetes in Adults) - a slowly evolving immune-mediated diabetes of adults; a hybrid form that presents as type 2 phenotype but has autoimmune features.
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: Compare age of onset between type 1 and type 2 diabetes.
cards.back: Type 1: Occurs in younger individuals, typically before age 35. Type 2: Occurs in older individuals, typically after age 35.
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: How does body composition differ between type 1 and type 2 diabetes?
cards.back: Type 1: Lean (due to lack of insulin for fat deposition). Type 2: Overweight (due to high insulin and insulin resistance, usually induced by overweight).
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What is the HLA association in type 1 diabetes?
cards.back: Type 1 diabetes is associated with HLA-DQA0-DQB (HLA-DR3-04). Type 2 diabetes has no HLA link.
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What is the concordance rate for type 1 and type 2 diabetes in monozygotic twins?
cards.back: Type 1: 30-35% concordance. Type 2: 90% concordance.
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: Name the autoimmune markers present in type 1 diabetes.
cards.back:
1) Glutamic acid decarboxylase antibody (anti-GAD)
2) ICA (islet cell antigen)
3) IA2 antibody
4) Insulin antibody
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What is the acute crisis presentation for untreated type 1 vs type 2 diabetes?
cards.back: Type 1: Rapidly progresses to diabetic ketoacidosis. Type 2: Usually presents as hyperosmolar hyperglycemic crisis (previously called hyperosmolar non-ketotic hyperglycemia).
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: Describe the normal fasting plasma glucose and 2-hour glucose on GTT according to WHO criteria.
cards.back: Normal: Fasting plasma glucose < 6.1 mmol/L AND 2-hour plasma glucose < 7.8 mmol/L on GTT.
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What defines impaired fasting glucose (IFG) according to WHO?
cards.back: Fasting plasma glucose 6.1-7 mmol/L AND 2-hour plasma glucose < 7.8 mmol/L on GTT (only fasting glucose is abnormal).
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What defines impaired glucose tolerance (IGT) according to WHO?
cards.back: Fasting plasma glucose < 7 mmol/L AND 2-hour plasma glucose 7.8-11.1 mmol/L on GTT.
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What are the ADA diagnostic criteria for diabetes using fasting plasma glucose?
cards.back: ADA normal: < 5.6 mmol/L. Impaired fasting glucose: 5.6-6.9 mmol/L. Diabetes: ≥ 7 mmol/L.
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What is the diagnostic threshold for diabetes on 2-hour glucose during GTT?
cards.back: Diabetes is diagnosed if 2-hour plasma glucose ≥ 11.1 mmol/L on GTT (both WHO and ADA).
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What are the four diagnostic tests for diabetes according to ADA 2010-current?
cards.back:
1) Fasting plasma glucose ≥ 7 mmol/L
2) 2-hour plasma glucose ≥ 11.1 mmol/L on GTT
3) HbA1c ≥ 6.5%
4) Random plasma glucose ≥ 11.1 mmol/L with classic symptoms or hypoglycemic crisis
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: When must a diagnosis of diabetes be confirmed?
cards.back: The diagnosis of diabetes must be confirmed by a repeat test on another day (can be same test or different test) in the absence of unequivocal hyperglycemia.
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What is the procedure for performing an oral glucose tolerance test (GTT)?
cards.back: No caloric intake for 8 hours (fasting). Then 75 grams of anhydrous glucose dissolved in water (or 250 moles of glucose) is given orally. Blood glucose is measured at fasting and 2 hours.
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What standardization is required for HbA1c testing?
cards.back: HbA1c must be done in a lab with a method certified by the National Glycohemoglobin Certification Program and standardized to the DCCT (Diabetes Control and Complications Trial) assay.
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What does ADA 2010 define as 'increased risk for diabetes'?
cards.back: Impaired fasting glucose: 5.6-6.1 mmol/L, OR IGT (2-hour: 7.8-11 mmol/L), OR intermediate HbA1c: 5.7-6.4%
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What are the screening recommendations for type 2 diabetes by age?
cards.back: Age ≥ 45: All should be screened with any method, especially if BMI ≥ 25. Age < 45: Screen if overweight (BMI ≥ 25) PLUS one other risk factor.
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: List risk factors for screening type 2 diabetes in people under 45.
cards.back: Physical inactivity, family history in first-degree relative, high-risk ethnicity (Pima Indians, Pacific Islanders, migrant Asian Indians), gestational diabetes, big babies (>4kg), IFG/IGT, hypertension, dyslipidemia, PCOS, vascular disease.
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What is the global burden of diabetes as of 2021 (10th edition IDF)?
cards.back: 537 million people worldwide with diabetes in 2021, projected to increase 46% to 783 million by 2045. Greatest increase in Africa (134% from 24M to 55M).
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: How does the burden of diabetes compare to other major diseases?
cards.back: More people have diabetes than HIV, tuberculosis, and malaria combined.
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What are the acute complications of diabetes?
cards.back: Metabolic: hyper/hypoglycemia, diabetic ketoacidosis. Infections: acute (pneumonia, arthritis, UTI) or chronic (tuberculosis).
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: Name the major microvascular complications of diabetes.
cards.back:
1) Retinopathy (90% of type 1, 20-90% of type 2)
2) Nephropathy (⅓ of type 1, 3-15% of type 2)
3) Neuropathy (10-100% of type 1 and 2)
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: Name the major macrovascular (atherosclerotic) complications of diabetes.
cards.back:
1) Coronary artery disease/cardiovascular disease (2-4 fold increased risk)
2) Cerebrovascular disease/stroke (2-fold increased risk)
3) Peripheral vascular disease
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What other chronic complications of diabetes exist beyond micro/macrovascular?
cards.back: Diabetic foot (combination of vascular, neuropathy, infection), cataracts, skin and connective tissue diseases, rheumatological disease.
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What is the relationship between hyperglycemia and microvascular complications?
cards.back: Microvascular complications are specific to diabetes and related to the duration and degree of hyperglycemia. Hyperglycemia is important for both initiation and progression of complications. Other factors (metabolic, genetic, vascular, environmental) contribute but hyperglycemia is the major determinant.
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: At what time do chronic complications develop in type 1 diabetes?
cards.back: Chronic complications occur years after diagnosis of type 1 diabetes (diagnosed usually via DKA presentation).
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: At what time do chronic complications develop in type 2 diabetes?
cards.back: Chronic complications may occur years after diagnosis, but in many cases complications may be the mode of presentation (found at first detection of diabetes).
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: Define diabetic nephropathy.
cards.back: Diabetic nephropathy is characterized by: 1) Persistent proteinuria, 2) Progressive decline in renal function (GFR), 3) Arterial hypertension, 4) Associated retinopathy
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What is the prevalence of diabetic nephropathy in type 1 vs type 2?
cards.back: Type 1: ⅓ (approximately 33%). Type 2: 15%.
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What is the most important factor for development of diabetic nephropathy?
cards.back: Duration of diabetes is the most important factor. In type 1, incidence peaks at about 15 years. In type 2, it can peak earlier.
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What are the causes of renal involvement in diabetes besides diabetic nephropathy?
cards.back: Infections (pyelonephritis), atherosclerosis causing renal vascular disease, nephrotoxic drugs (amikacin), and radio contrast injury
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: What is the percentage of diabetic patients who may develop proteinuria unrelated to diabetes?
cards.back: 3 to 8% of diabetic patients may develop proteinuria that is not related to diabetes
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: What clinical findings indicate the need for renal biopsy in a diabetic patient?
cards.back: Microscopic hematuria, sudden onset of proteinuria or nephrotic syndrome, red cell casts in urine, rapid decline in renal function, short duration type 1 diabetes (<5 years), or absence of retinopathy
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: Define established diabetic nephropathy in terms of proteinuria quantification
cards.back: Persistent proteinuria where urine protein excretion is dipstick detectable (>500 mg or 0.5g per day) or urine albumin excretion >300 mg/day, or >200 micrograms/minute on timed specimen
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: What is microalbuminuria and what is its definition in diabetic nephropathy?
cards.back: Also called incipient diabetic nephropathy; albumin excretion rate between 30-300 mg/day over 24 hours, or 20-200 micrograms/minute on timed sample (e.g., 12-hour overnight)
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: What percentage of type 1 diabetics develop glomerulosclerosis after 10 years, and what percentage develop diabetic nephropathy clinically?
cards.back: Glomerulosclerosis develops in >90% of type 1 diabetes after 10 years, but diabetic nephropathy (with persistent proteinuria, renal decline, hypertension, retinopathy) only develops in 35-40%
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: Name the 5 stages of diabetic nephropathy and their timeframes
cards.back: Stage 1: Glomerular hyperfiltration (onset, 2-3 years); Stage 2: Silent stage; Stage 3: Incipient nephropathy/microalbuminuria (>5 years); Stage 4: Established/overt nephropathy; Stage 5: End-stage renal failure (20+ years)
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: At which stage of diabetic nephropathy does urine albumin excretion become abnormal?
cards.back: Only at stage 3 (incipient nephropathy); persistent proteinuria appears from stage 4 onward
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: Describe the pattern of GFR changes across the 5 stages of diabetic nephropathy
cards.back: GFR is increased in stages 1-3 (hyperfiltration through incipient stage), starts decreasing at onset of persistent proteinuria (stage 4), and continues declining with serum creatinine rising in stage 5
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: When does hypertension typically manifest in diabetic nephropathy?
cards.back: Blood pressure is still normal in stage 3; hypertension develops only when overt nephropathy appears (stage 4) and persists into stage 5
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: When does retinopathy appear in diabetic nephropathy?
cards.back: Retinopathy starts developing only after the onset of established nephropathy (stage 4); nothing appears up to stage 3
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: What is the rate of decline of GFR once established nephropathy develops?
cards.back: GFR declines at approximately 1 mL/min per month (12 mL/min per year); untreated patients progress to end-stage renal disease in about 7 years from onset
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: What is the clinical importance of early detection of incipient nephropathy?
cards.back: Early treatment of incipient nephropathy prevents and delays the onset of overt nephropathy and end-stage renal failure; treatment after overt nephropathy develops prevents or decreases progression to ESRD
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: List the methods for detecting diabetic nephropathy clinically
cards.back: Blood pressure elevation (especially in type 1), urine albumin excretion (micro vs. persistent), GFR measurement (eGFR, creatinine clearance, isotope), serum/urine creatinine, diminishing insulin requirements, recurrent hypoglycemia in insulin-treated patients
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: What are the CKD stages based on GFR according to the transcript?
cards.back: Stage 1: GFR >90; Stage 2: 60-89; Stage 3: 45-59; Stage 3b: 30-44; Stage 4: 15-29 (severely diminished); Stage 5: <15 or dialysis-requiring (kidney failure)
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: What are the management goals for diabetic nephropathy?
cards.back: Prevent or delay renal disease progression, improve clinical outcomes, prolong survival, decrease mortality, delay time to dialysis or renal transplant
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: Which factor is more important for preventing progression to ESRD in established nephropathy: glycemic control or hypertension control?
cards.back: Hypertension control is more important than glycemic control for preventing progression to ESRD once overt nephropathy develops
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: What dietary protein restriction is recommended in diabetic nephropathy management?
cards.back: 0.8 to 1.0 g/kg body weight per day
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: What serum creatinine level triggers assessment for chronic renal program?
cards.back: Serum creatinine ≥500 micromoles/L (or at lower levels like 250 micromoles/L in some settings); GFR <20 mL/min also triggers referral
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: Which antidiabetic agents are contraindicated in renal impairment?
cards.back: Metformin is contraindicated if GFR <70 mL/min; sulfonylureas (long-acting like glibenclamide) are contraindicated, but short-acting agents like gliclazide can be used until GFR <30 mL/min when all oral agents stop
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: What drugs are first-line for hypertension and proteinuria in diabetic nephropathy?
cards.back: ACE inhibitors or, if side effects (cough), angiotensin receptor blockers (ARBs); can also use calcium antagonists, loop diuretics, alpha blockers, or beta blockers
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: At what level of renal dysfunction are ACE inhibitors/ARBs used?
cards.back: For persistent albuminuria (micro or overt), whether normal, elevated, or reduced kidney function; even when creatinine >133 micromoles/L
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: Describe the eye involvement in diabetes beyond retinopathy
cards.back: Cataracts (posterior subcapsular or snowflake), neuropathies (optic neuropathy, papillopathy, ocular nerve palsies of CN III, IV, VI, VII), autonomic pupillary dysfunction, and other retino-vascular diseases (arterial/venous occlusions), vitreous disease (hemorrhage, asteroidosis)
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: What are the 3 categories of diabetic retinopathy classification?
cards.back: Background retinopathy, pre-proliferative retinopathy, and proliferative retinopathy
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: What are the hallmark lesions of background diabetic retinopathy?
cards.back: Microaneurysms, dot and blot hemorrhages, hard exudates
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: What are the features of pre-proliferative retinopathy?
cards.back: Venous anomalies (beading, looping, duplication), intraretinal microvascular abnormalities (IRMAs), cotton wool spots (>5), deep round hemorrhages
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: What characterizes proliferative diabetic retinopathy?
cards.back: New vessel formation/neovascularization at the disc (NVD) or elsewhere (NVE); these fragile vessels hemorrhage leading to preretinal/subhyaloid hemorrhages, vitreous hemorrhage, fibrosis, retinal detachment, or iris involvement causing rubeosis iridis and glaucoma
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: What is maculopathy and how is it identified clinically?
cards.back: Macular edema and hard exudates at the macula; identified by asking patient to look directly into ophthalmoscope (reaches macula) or by dilating eye and looking lateral to disc; presents as macular edema or macular star pattern
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: What are the management options for diabetic retinopathy?
cards.back: Glycemic control, blood pressure control, lipid control; specific treatments include laser photocoagulation for high-risk proliferative retinopathy or severe nonproliferative retinopathy with macular edema; vitrectomy and cataract extraction for complicated cases
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: What are the 3 types of diabetic neuropathy?
cards.back: Hypoglycemic neuropathy (reversible), symmetrical polyneuropathy (acute painful or chronic sensory/motor/autonomic), focal and multifocal neuropathies (cranial nerve palsies, carpal tunnel, proximal motor neuropathy/diabetic amyotrophy, thoracoabdominal)
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: What is hypoglycemic neuropathy and is it reversible?
cards.back: Reversible reduction in nerve conduction with transient sensory symptoms occurring with hypoglycemic episodes
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: Describe acute symmetrical polyneuropathy in diabetes
cards.back: Painful neuropathy often presenting with burning feet, especially at night; reversible with glycemic control
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: What is chronic symmetrical polyneuropathy in diabetes?
cards.back: Can be purely sensory, sensorimotor, or autonomic; typically irreversible
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: List the focal and multifocal neuropathies in diabetes
cards.back: Entrapment neuropathies (carpal tunnel), cranial nerve palsies (CN III, IV, VI, VII), proximal motor neuropathy (diabetic amyotrophy), thoracoabdominal neuropathy
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: What are the common symptoms of peripheral diabetic neuropathy?
cards.back: Paresthesias, pain, sensory loss, burning feet; first sensation lost is vibration sense
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: What are the symptoms of autonomic diabetic neuropathy?
cards.back: Gastroparesis, hyperhidrosis, anhidrosis, nocturnal diarrhea, erectile dysfunction, postural hypotension, GI bloating, hypoglycemia unawareness, cardiac/vascular effects, bladder problems
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: How is peripheral diabetic neuropathy examined clinically?
cards.back: Testing touch with monofilament and reflexes; documenting vibration sense loss (first to be involved); for autonomic neuropathy, test postural hypotension and fixed tachycardia
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: What special tests are used to diagnose diabetic neuropathy?
cards.back: Biothesiometer, cardiovascular reflex response, nerve conduction studies, nerve biopsy
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: What tests assess autonomic neuropathy in diabetes?
cards.back: Heart rate variation during deep breathing, heart rate increase on standing, change during Valsalva maneuver, postural fall in systolic blood pressure
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: What pharmacological agents are used for painful diabetic neuropathy?
cards.back: Tricyclic antidepressants (amitriptyline, imipramine), anticonvulsants (gabapentin, carbamazepine, pregabalin), duloxetine (serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor), capsaicin (substance P inhibitor)
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: How is gastroparesis managed in diabetic autonomic neuropathy?
cards.back: Dietary changes and prokinetics such as metoclopramide or domperidone
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: What are the treatment options for erectile dysfunction in diabetic neuropathy?
cards.back: Phosphodiesterase type 5 inhibitors (sildenafil/Viagra), prostaglandins (intracorporeal or urethral), vacuum devices, penile prosthesis
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: How is gastric hyperhidrosis managed in diabetic autonomic neuropathy?
cards.back: Glycopyrrolate or topical aluminum preparations
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: What is the recommended follow-up schedule for diabetic complications screening?
cards.back: At each visit: assess symptoms, measure weight/waist, BMI, blood pressure, urine dipstick for protein, self-monitoring blood glucose; every 3 months (or 6 if difficult); annual review for chronic complications; more frequently if problems arise
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: What vital parameters and measurements should be recorded at each diabetes visit?
cards.back: Weight, waist circumference, BMI, blood pressure, urine protein on dipstick, self-monitored blood glucose
cards.source: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014
cards.sourceFile: Diagnosis  Classification Of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-011-014.yml

cards.front: What are the two main parts of the pituitary gland?
cards.back: The anterior pituitary and posterior pituitary.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What is the typical weight of the pituitary gland?
cards.back: 0.4 to 0.8 grams. Despite its small size, it has the highest blood flow of any organ in the body.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What are the main presentations of pituitary disorders?
cards.back: Hypersecretion of hormones, visual disorders, enlargement of the sella turcica (space-occupying lesions), and hypopituitarism.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What broad categories of investigation are used for pituitary disorders?
cards.back: Radiology, endocrine/biochemical tests, and neuro-ophthalmic (visual field) evaluation.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What is the difference between gigantism and acromegaly?
cards.back: Gigantism occurs when excess growth hormone develops before closure of the epiphyses (growth plates) in bones; acromegaly occurs after epiphyseal closure.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What percentage of acromegaly cases present as macroadenomas at diagnosis?
cards.back: 75% of individuals have macroadenomas (>10 mm) at the time of diagnosis.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: List the facial features characteristic of acromegaly.
cards.back: Coarse features, prominent supraorbital ridges (from enlarged frontal sinuses), prognathism (forward protrusion of lower jaw), reversal of bite, separation of teeth, thickened lips and nose, and macroglossia.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What are the main skeletal manifestations of acromegaly in the hands and feet?
cards.back: Hands: enlargement (spade-like), compression of nerves. Feet: large feet with proximal myopathy, osteoarthritis, and carpal/tarsal tunnel syndrome.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What visual disturbance is characteristic of acromegaly?
cards.back: Bitemporal hemianopia due to pressure on the optic chiasm from the expanding tumor.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What organ enlargements occur in acromegaly due to IGF-1 stimulation?
cards.back: Cardiomegaly, goiter (thyroid), splenomegaly, enlarged liver and kidneys, and laryngeal hypertrophy. This can lead to secondary hypertension and increased risk of carcinoma of the colon.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What metabolic abnormalities are seen in acromegaly?
cards.back: Hypertension (develops in 20-40%), glucose intolerance/pre-diabetes, and diabetes (13-20%).
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What skin changes occur in acromegaly?
cards.back: Oily skin, hirsutism, acanthosis nigricans, hypertricosis, and skin tags. >3 skin tags are associated with colonic polyps or carcinoma.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What complications can result from laryngeal hypertrophy in acromegaly?
cards.back: Obstructive sleep apnea, which may cause somnolence and reduce life expectancy.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What causes shortened lifespan in untreated acromegaly?
cards.back: Death may result from cardiovascular disease, cerebrovascular disease, respiratory disease, or associated malignancy (colon cancer).
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What radiological investigations are performed for acromegaly?
cards.back: Plain X-rays of skull (showing enlarged pituitary fossa, erosion of clinoid processes, enlarged frontal sinuses), hands (tufting of terminal phalanges, widened joint spaces), feet (increased heel pad thickness), and MRI of the pituitary.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What does the plain X-ray of the feet show in acromegaly?
cards.back: Increased heel pad thickness (beyond normal limits for sex), widening of joint spaces, and osteoarthritis.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What is the purpose of the MRI of the pituitary in acromegaly?
cards.back: To determine tumor location (intrasellar vs. suprasellar extension), size (microadenoma vs. macroadenoma), and extent of extension, which guides treatment decisions.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What are the basal biochemical tests for acromegaly?
cards.back: Elevated serum growth hormone, elevated IGF-1, elevated prolactin (if co-secreting tumor), elevated serum phosphate, hyperglycemia/dysglycemia, and elevated serum calcium.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What is the gold standard provocative test for acromegaly?
cards.back: Oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT). In normal individuals, glucose rises and growth hormone falls. In acromegaly, baseline growth hormone is high and fails to decrease with rising glucose levels.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: How is pituitary reserve assessed in acromegaly?
cards.back: Insulin tolerance test (gold standard) to assess cortisol response, TRH test for TSH response, GNRH test for LH/FSH response, or synacthen test (1 microgram IV) and glucagon test for cortisol.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: How is posterior pituitary function assessed in acromegaly?
cards.back: Basal test: serum and urine osmolality. Provocative test: water deprivation test. This evaluates for cranial diabetes insipidus or arginine vasopressin deficiency.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What are the objectives of acromegaly management?
cards.back: Reduce growth hormone and IGF-1 to normal levels, decrease tumor size, and preserve anterior and posterior pituitary function.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What is the first-line treatment for acromegaly?
cards.back: Surgery: transphenoidal pituitary/adenoma resection, usually via the nose. Either targeted or total resection depending on suprasellar extension.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: When is radiotherapy indicated for acromegaly?
cards.back: When the patient refuses surgery, is a poor surgical risk, or when surgery fails. Includes external irradiation and stereotactic radiotherapy.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What medical therapies are used for acromegaly?
cards.back: Dopamine agonists (bromocriptine, cabergoline); somatostatin analogs (octreotide, pasireotide); growth hormone receptor antagonists (pegvisomant). Used if surgery refused/failed, or as adjunct to radiotherapy.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What is the bromocriptine dosing for acromegaly?
cards.back: 2.5 mg three times daily, up to 60 mg per day.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What are the cure rates for acromegaly after transphenoidal surgery?
cards.back: 80% with microadenoma; 50% with macroadenoma (>10 mm). All patients require long-term follow-up for recurrence.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: Why is prolactinoma the most common functioning pituitary tumor?
