cards.back: It secretes prolactin, and occurs in both micro- and macroadenomas. Microadenomas are more common in females (70%), while macroadenomas are more common in males (60%).
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What are the clinical features of prolactinoma?
cards.back: Mass-related (headache, cranial nerve palsies, visual field defects) and hormone-related: galactorrhea (~80% of hyperprolactinemia), amenorrhea/infertility (women), gynecomastia/decreased libido/impotence (men), acne, hirsutism, and osteoporosis (both).
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What is the diagnostic approach for prolactinoma?
cards.back: Radiological: MRI of pituitary. Biochemical: basal serum prolactin (no provocative tests needed). If basal prolactin is >4× upper limit of normal, prolactinoma is the likely cause of hyperprolactinemia.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What is the first-line management of prolactinoma?
cards.back: Medical therapy with dopamine agonists (bromocriptine or analogs like cabergoline or quinagolide). These result in tumor involution.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: When is surgery indicated for prolactinoma?
cards.back: Surgery is rarely indicated. Microadenomas respond well to medical therapy and rarely present. Macroadenomas respond poorly to surgery but may be required for sudden, massive deterioration of vision.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What is the difference between isolated hypopituitarism and pan-hypopituitarism?
cards.back: Isolated hypopituitarism: only one hormone deficiency (e.g., ACTH, gonadotropins, TSH, or growth hormone deficiency). Pan-hypopituitarism: deficiency of multiple pituitary hormones.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What are the main causes of hypopituitarism (deficiency of pituitary hormones)?
cards.back: Idiopathic (autoimmune), congenital (Simmons disease, Rathke's cyst), tumors (primary or secondary), vascular (Sheehan syndrome, pituitary apoplexy), inflammatory (granulomatous, tuberculosis, sarcoidosis, syphilis), infective (tuberculosis, fungal, parasitic, pneumocystis), traumatic, radiotherapy, depositional (hemochromatosis), and primary empty sella.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What is Sheehan syndrome?
cards.back: Postpartum necrosis of the pituitary gland occurring in the setting of hemorrhage with systemic hypotension, resulting in vasospasm and pituitary destruction. Occurs after severe postpartum bleeding.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What is pituitary apoplexy?
cards.back: Sudden infarction of the pituitary gland caused by interruption of blood supply to a large tumor or when the tumor outstrips its blood supply. Results in rapid pituitary necrosis.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What specific hormone deficiencies occur in idiopathic hypopituitarism and what is the proposed mechanism?
cards.back: Isolated growth hormone, gonadotropin, TSH, or ACTH deficiencies. The mechanism is thought to be autoimmune.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What are the congenital causes of hypopituitarism?
cards.back: Simmons disease (under or co-development of the pituitary gland) and persistence of Rathke's cyst.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What primary hypothalamic and pituitary tumors can cause hypopituitarism?
cards.back: Craniopharyngioma, chromophobe non-functioning adenomas, and functioning adenomas of the pituitary.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What are examples of secondary tumors that can cause hypopituitarism?
cards.back: Carcinomas from the lung (bronchus), breast, and other sites that metastasize to the pituitary.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What is the typical order of hormone involvement in hypopituitarism, using Sheehan syndrome as the gold standard example?
cards.back: Growth hormone first, then prolactin, then gonadotropins, followed many years later by TSH, and finally ACTH (least likely to be deficient).
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What are the clinical features of growth hormone deficiency in hypopituitarism in children?
cards.back: Failure to grow and short stature due to growth hormone deficiency.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What are the clinical features of prolactin deficiency in postpartum hypopituitarism (Sheehan syndrome)?
cards.back: Failure to lactate in the immediate postpartum period due to prolactin deficiency.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What are the signs of gonadotropin deficiency in women with hypopituitarism?
cards.back: Amenorrhea and infertility (occurs a few months after hormone deficiency develops).
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What are the late manifestations of untreated hypopituitarism?
cards.back: Patients may develop coma due to hypoglycemia (from growth hormone deficiency), ACTH deficiency (adrenal crisis), hypothermia (from diminished TSH), or hypo-adrenal state. ACTH deficiency is only glucocorticoid-related, not mineralocorticoid-related.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What are the radiological investigations for hypopituitarism?
cards.back: Plain X-ray of the skull and MRI of the pituitary to assess for tumors or anatomical abnormalities.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What basal biochemical tests are performed for anterior pituitary function in hypopituitarism?
cards.back: Serum growth hormone, IGF-1, prolactin, LH, FSH, estradiol/testosterone, TSH, free T3, free T4, serum ACTH, and serum cortisol.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What basal biochemical tests assess posterior pituitary function?
cards.back: Serum osmolality and urine osmolality to evaluate ADH (arginine vasopressin) function.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What provocative tests are used for anterior pituitary assessment in hypopituitarism?
cards.back: Insulin tolerance test (to assess cortisol, growth hormone, and prolactin response to hypoglycemia), GnRH test (for LH/FSH), TRH test (for TSH and prolactin), synacthen test (for cortisol), glucagon test (for cortisol and growth hormone), and L-dopa test (for growth hormone).
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What provocative test is used for posterior pituitary assessment in hypopituitarism?
cards.back: Water deprivation test to evaluate ADH response and distinguish cranial diabetes insipidus from other causes of polyuria.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What is the hormone replacement approach for growth hormone deficiency in hypopituitarism?
cards.back: Somatotropin given daily subcutaneously.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What is the hormone replacement for glucocorticoid deficiency in hypopituitarism?
cards.back: Hydrocortisone.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What is the hormone replacement for thyroid deficiency in hypopituitarism and what is the critical caveat?
cards.back: Levothyroxine 100-200 micrograms daily. CRITICAL: Never replace thyroxine without first giving cortisol, as thyroxine accelerates cortisol degradation and may precipitate a hypo-adrenal crisis.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: How is hypogonadism managed in women with hypopituitarism?
cards.back: In women with intact uterus: estrogen-progesterone combination. In women without uterus: estrogen only. For fertility, must use gonadotropins (HCG or gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists).
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: How is hypogonadism managed in men with hypopituitarism?
cards.back: Testosterone esters given as transdermal patch or intramuscular injection. For fertility, gonadotropins required.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: How is fertility achieved in hypopituitarism when sex hormone replacement alone is insufficient?
cards.back: Use gonadotropins: HCG (human chorionic gonadotropin) given three times per week, or gonadotropin-releasing hormone (gonadotropin-releasing hormone) if the patient has hypothalamic dysfunction.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What is the typical presentation and management consideration for craniopharyngioma as a cause of hypopituitarism in children?
cards.back: Most common cause of short stature due to pituitary-hypothalamic problems in children. Often presents with growth failure and requires growth hormone replacement after treatment.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What are the two components of posterior pituitary hormone deficiency affecting water balance?
cards.back: Deficiency of anti-diuretic hormone (ADH), also called vasopressin or arginine vasopressin (AVD), which regulates water reabsorption in the kidneys.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What is the current nomenclature for deficiency of ADH?
cards.back: Previously called cranial diabetes insipidus; renamed in 2022 to arginine vasopressin deficiency (AVD). The term "diabetes insipidus" is still commonly used.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What is the anatomical basis of cranial diabetes insipidus?
cards.back: ADH is produced in the hypothalamus (paraventricular and supraoptic nuclei), travels via the hypothalamic-pituitary tract, and is stored in the posterior pituitary before release. Damage at any of these sites (hypothalamus, tract, or posterior pituitary) can cause cranial diabetes insipidus.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What is the "rule of thirds" for the etiology of cranial diabetes insipidus?
cards.back: One-third due to tumors (hypothalamic or pituitary), one-third idiopathic, and one-third miscellaneous (infections, trauma, etc.).
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: In which conditions does isolated ADH deficiency (without other anterior pituitary hormone abnormalities) occur?
cards.back: Only two conditions: eosinophilic granuloma (osteocytosis X) and traumatic head injury (e.g., motor vehicle accident). Otherwise, ADH deficiency typically occurs with other anterior pituitary hormone deficiencies.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What are the primary hypothalamic and pituitary tumors that cause cranial diabetes insipidus?
cards.back: Craniopharyngioma (most common hypothalamic tumor) and pituitary tumors; secondary tumors include those from breast, bronchus, and melanoma.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What is the first symptom of cranial diabetes insipidus in children?
cards.back: Polyuria (excessive urine output), typically 4-20 liters per day with dilute urine. In some cases, polydipsia (excessive thirst) occurs first in adults.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What complications can result from untreated cranial diabetes insipidus?
cards.back: Dehydration, weight loss, constipation, pyrexia (fever), and if ACTH is also deficient, these symptoms may be masked due to cortisol's role in water excretion. Polyuria may only manifest after cortisol replacement.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What compressive symptoms can occur with tumors causing cranial diabetes insipidus?
cards.back: Eye involvement (bitemporal hemianopia from optic chiasm compression) and cranial nerve palsies.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: How does concurrent ACTH deficiency affect the presentation of cranial diabetes insipidus?
cards.back: Cortisol is important for excretion of water load. With ACTH deficiency (cortisol deficiency), the polyuria may be masked, and diabetes insipidus may only become evident after cortisol replacement with hydrocortisone.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What basal investigations are performed for suspected cranial diabetes insipidus?
cards.back: Serum osmolality (elevated), urine osmolality (low), and urine specific gravity (<1.005). Dipstick urinalysis shows low specific gravity.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What does the water deprivation test demonstrate in cranial diabetes insipidus?
cards.back: In normal individuals, fluid restriction maintains serum osmolality at the expense of urine osmolality. In diabetes insipidus, serum osmolality remains very high despite fluid restriction and only normalizes with exogenous (intranasal) ADH (desmopressin).
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What are the differential diagnoses for cranial diabetes insipidus?
cards.back: Arginine vasopressin deficiency (cranial diabetes insipidus) vs. arginine vasopressin resistance (nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, which may be congenital or acquired from renal disease).
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What other conditions present with polyuria similar to diabetes insipidus?
cards.back: Diabetes mellitus (urine specific gravity is high due to glucose), chronic renal failure, psychogenic polydipsia (compulsive water drinking), and electrolyte abnormalities (hypokalemia, hyperkalemia) including Conn syndrome or hyperparathyroidism.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What is the first-line treatment for cranial diabetes insipidus?
cards.back: Desmopressin (DDAVP - synthetic ADH) given as intranasal spray, oral tablets, or in acute settings via subcutaneous or intravenous injection. Intranasal is no longer commonly used; oral tablets are standard.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What are alternative agents that were historically used for diabetes insipidus when desmopressin was unavailable?
cards.back: The "four Cs": chlorpropamide, carbamazepine, chlorothiazide, and proprietary ADH-enhancing drugs. With desmopressin now available, these are rarely used.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What is syndrome of inappropriate ADH (SIADH)?
cards.back: Characterized by hyponatremia (low serum sodium) without edema, due to inappropriately excessive secretion of ADH in the presence of low serum osmolality.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What tumors can cause SIADH?
cards.back: Small cell lung carcinoma (most common), other bronchial carcinomas, duodenal/pancreatic carcinoma, gastric carcinoma, prostatic carcinoma, bronchial carcinoid, thymomas, and hematologic malignancies (lymphoma, leukemia, Hodgkin's disease).
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What drugs can cause SIADH?
cards.back: Desmopressin (DDAVP), oxytocin, the "four Cs" (chlorpromidine, clofibrate, carbamazepine, chlorothiazide), antipsychotics (phenothiazines), tricyclic antidepressants, oncology drugs (vincristine, cyclophosphamide), and miscellaneous drugs (nicotine, NSAIDs).
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What pulmonary conditions can cause SIADH?
cards.back: Tuberculosis (most common non-malignant cause), lung abscess, pneumonia, empyema, and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What neurological conditions can cause SIADH?
cards.back: Head trauma, skull fractures, subdural hematoma, subarachnoid hemorrhage, cerebrovascular thrombosis, cerebral atrophy, acute meningitis, tuberculosis meningitis, pyogenic meningitis, Guillain-Barre syndrome, and lupus encephalitis.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What miscellaneous conditions can cause SIADH?
cards.back: Hypothyroidism and positive pressure ventilation (mechanical ventilation).
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What are the clinical features of mild hyponatremia (serum sodium 130-135 mEq/L) in SIADH?
cards.back: Patients may be asymptomatic or present with anorexia, nausea, vomiting, lethargy, and weight gain.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What are the clinical features of severe acute hyponatremia (<120 mEq/L) in SIADH?
cards.back: Worsening lethargy, weight gain, confusion, convulsions, coma, and death if untreated. Importantly, edema and hypertension are rare in SIADH.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: Why is the rate of sodium decline important in SIADH?
cards.back: Rapid decline in serum sodium causes more severe symptoms than gradual decline at the same final sodium level.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What are the diagnostic criteria for SIADH?
cards.back: Serum sodium <130 mEq/L, low serum osmolality with elevated urine osmolality, urine sodium elevated (>40 mEq/L), and absence of hypotension, edema, or dehydration. Elevated plasma ADH (if measurable) confirms diagnosis.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What biochemical abnormalities are seen in SIADH?
cards.back: Low serum urea, creatinine, and uric acid; low albumin; low bicarbonate and potassium; low serum osmolality; and elevated urine osmolality and urine sodium.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What are the three types of hyponatremia based on volume status?
cards.back: Type 1 (hypervolemic): cardiac failure, liver disease, renal disease. Type 2 (hypovolemic): dehydration from diarrhea or vomiting. Type 3 (euvolemic): SIADH (no edema, no hypertension).
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What other causes of low serum sodium must be considered besides SIADH?
cards.back: Primary polydipsia, hypotension, pseudo-hyponatremia (from hyperlipidemia or hyperproteinemia causing laboratory artifact), and hyperglycemia.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What is the management goal for SIADH?
cards.back: Increase serum sodium toward normal (>135 mEq/L) slowly and identify the underlying cause.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What is the fluid restriction for mild asymptomatic SIADH?
cards.back: Restrict fluid to approximately 500 mL per day.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What is the treatment for severe symptomatic SIADH?
cards.back: Hypertonic saline (3-5% saline) given carefully, with other agents like tolvaptan (vasopressin antagonist), lithium, glucocorticoids, and demeclocycline available as alternatives.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What is the critical safety consideration when correcting hyponatremia in SIADH?
cards.back: Increase serum sodium slowly—no more than 12 mEq/L per day. Rapid correction can cause central pontine myelinolysis (CPM), a devastating neurological condition characterized by quadriplegia, ataxia, and abnormal eye movements with high mortality.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What is central pontine myelinolysis (CPM)?
cards.back: A serious neurological complication of too-rapid sodium correction in hyponatremia, characterized by quadriplegia, ataxia, extraocular muscle weakness, and abnormal ocular movements, with very high mortality.
cards.source: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012
cards.sourceFile: Disorders Of The Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1 - 2026_pages-011-012.yml

cards.front: What are the three hormones involved in calcium homeostasis?
cards.back: PTH (parathyroid hormone), vitamin D, and calcitonin
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What are the three main organs involved in calcium homeostasis?
cards.back: Bone, gut (GI tract), and kidneys
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: Describe the action of PTH on calcium and phosphate
cards.back: PTH acts on bone and kidney, resulting in increased calcium and decreased phosphate
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: Describe the action of vitamin D on calcium and phosphate
cards.back: Vitamin D acts on bone and GI tract, increasing both calcium and phosphate
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What is the net effect of calcitonin on serum calcium and phosphate?
cards.back: Calcitonin causes decreased calcium and decreased phosphate
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: Which form of calcium is physiologically important?
cards.back: Free or ionized calcium (not bound to albumin)
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: How is corrected serum calcium calculated?
cards.back: (40 - serum albumin) × 0.02 + measured serum calcium
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What defines primary hyperparathyroidism?
cards.back: Autonomous/excessive PTH secretion with elevated calcium and elevated PTH
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What are the causes of primary hyperparathyroidism?
cards.back: Adenoma (solitary or multiple), hyperplasia, rarely carcinoma. May be part of MEN 1 or MEN 2
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What defines secondary hyperparathyroidism?
cards.back: Compensatory increase in PTH due to low calcium. Causes: chronic renal disease, malabsorption, rickets
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What is tertiary hyperparathyroidism?
cards.back: Autonomous PTH secretion developing after prolonged secondary hyperparathyroidism. Elevated PTH with elevated calcium
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What is ectopic hyperparathyroidism?
cards.back: PTH-related protein secretion from malignancy. High calcium with low or absent measurable PTH
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What are the renal manifestations of primary hyperparathyroidism?
cards.back: Nephrolithiasis, nephrocalcinosis, hypertension, polyuria, polydipsia, renal tubular acidosis, Fanconi syndrome
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What is the relationship between nephrocalcinosis and hypercalcemia?
cards.back: Hypercalcemia causes inability to concentrate urine (nephrogenic diabetes insipidus) and renal tubular dysfunction
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What are the bone manifestations of primary hyperparathyroidism?
cards.back: Osteitis fibrosa cystica, brown tumors, pathological fractures, subperiosteal reabsorption
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: Which bones are classically affected by osteitis fibrosa cystica?
cards.back: Mandible, humerus, femur, clavicle. Subperiosteal reabsorption typically on right middle finger (radial aspect)
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What GI manifestations can hypercalcemia cause?
cards.back: Anorexia, nausea, weight loss, dyspepsia, peptic ulcer, abdominal colic, obstipation (obstinate constipation), pancreatitis
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What is the relationship between hypercalcemia and pancreatitis?
cards.back: Bidirectional: pancreatitis causes low calcium, while hyperparathyroidism can cause pancreatitis
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What mental manifestations can hypercalcemia cause?
cards.back: Anxiety, irritability, depression - often first to resolve after successful parathyroidectomy
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What ophthalmologic findings occur in hyperparathyroidism?
cards.back: Band keratopathy (corneal calcium deposits), pseudogout/chondrocalcinosis in joints
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What is acute hypercalcemia?
cards.back: Medical emergency: serum calcium ≥3.5-3.7 mmol/L with shock-like state, oliguria, dehydration, pyrexia, coma
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What are key laboratory findings in primary hyperparathyroidism?
cards.back: Elevated serum calcium, low phosphate, increased urinary calcium, elevated PTH, elevated alkaline phosphatase
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What acid-base abnormality occurs in primary hyperparathyroidism?
cards.back: Hypochloremic alkalosis (high bicarb, low chloride)
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What radiological features are seen in primary hyperparathyroidism?
cards.back: Subperiosteal reabsorption, salt-and-pepper skull appearance, cysts in bones, brown tumors, vertebral sclerosis
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: How does PTH affect cortical vs cancellous bone in hyperparathyroidism?
cards.back: Cortical bone (distal radius) affected MORE than cancellous bone (spine)
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What is sestamibi scan used for?
cards.back: Radionuclide imaging to localize parathyroid adenomas
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What ECG finding is seen in hyperparathyroidism?
cards.back: Shortened corrected QT interval (normal 0.35-0.40 seconds)
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What intraoperative test confirms successful parathyroidectomy?
cards.back: Dramatic reduction in PTH levels after adenoma resection
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What is the treatment of choice for primary hyperparathyroidism?
cards.back: Surgical treatment with PTH monitoring. For adenoma: resect adenoma. For hyperplasia: remove 3.5 glands
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What medical therapy is used for primary hyperparathyroidism when surgery not possible?
cards.back: Calcimimetic agents (cinacalcet) targeting calcium sensing receptors to suppress PTH
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: How is secondary hyperparathyroidism treated?
cards.back: If malabsorption: vitamin D. If renal disease: vitamin D or alpha-calcidiol
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What is the acute management of severe hypercalcemia?
cards.back: Rehydration with normal saline, bisphosphonates (alendronate or ibandronate), steroids for myeloma
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: How is hypoparathyroidism defined?
cards.back: Inadequate PTH secretion or resistance to PTH, causing low calcium and high phosphate
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What are the causes of hypoparathyroidism?
cards.back: Hereditary (idiopathic, polyglandular autoimmune, Albright syndrome), acquired (surgical, radiation), infiltration (TB, amyloid, sarcoid), hypermagnesemia
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What are the neuromuscular manifestations of hypoparathyroidism?
cards.back: Tetany, paresthesias, muscle cramps, Trousseau sign (carpopedal spasm), Chvostek sign (facial twitch), brisk reflexes, convulsions
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What is Trousseau sign?
cards.back: Blood pressure cuff inflated above systolic for 3 minutes causes carpopedal spasm - indicates latent tetany
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What is Chvostek sign and its significance?
cards.back: Tapping facial nerve over parotid induces facial contraction. Present in ~5% of normal population so NOT specific
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What CNS complications occur in hypoparathyroidism?
cards.back: Cataracts, benign intracranial hypertension (bilateral papilledema), epilepsy, basal ganglia calcification
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What is FAHR syndrome?
cards.back: Familial Hypoparathyroidism with basal ganglia calcification and Extrapyramidal symptoms
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What dermatological findings are seen in hypoparathyroidism?
cards.back: Dry scaly skin, eczema, alopecia, brittle deformed nails prone to paronychia/candida, malformed teeth if early onset
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What are the laboratory findings in hypoparathyroidism?
cards.back: Low serum calcium, high phosphate, low/absent PTH, decreased urinary calcium and phosphate, possible low magnesium
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What ECG finding is seen in hypoparathyroidism?
cards.back: Prolonged corrected QT interval (opposite of hyperparathyroidism)
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: How is transient hypoparathyroidism treated?
cards.back: Oral calcium carbonate, or intravenous calcium gluconate if severe
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: How is chronic hypoparathyroidism treated?
cards.back: Vitamin D analogs (1-alpha, alpha-calcidiol), calcitriol, teriparatide (PTH) if available. Treat hypomagnesemia
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What is the prognosis of hypoparathyroidism?
cards.back: Post-operative transient: good. Early detection: good. Late detection: worse
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What is pseudohypoparathyroidism (PHP)?
cards.back: Normal/hypoplastic parathyroid glands but target organs (kidney, bone) resist PTH. Biochemistry of hypoparathyroidism
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What causes pseudohypoparathyroidism?
cards.back: Deficiency of alpha subunit of G protein (Gα), causing end-organ PTH resistance
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What is Albright hereditary osteodystrophy?
cards.back: PHP phenotype: short stature, skeletal abnormalities, round face with frontal bossing, short metacarpals/phalanges
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What skeletal features characterize PHP?
cards.back: Short 4th and 5th metacarpals, short phalanges, short stubby hands, heterotopic ossifications, basal ganglia calcification
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What neurological features can PHP have?
cards.back: Mental deficiency, epilepsy, tetany, basal ganglia calcification (like hypoparathyroidism)
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: How is short 4th metacarpal detected clinically?
cards.back: Patient makes a fist - normally all metacarpal heads level. In PHP, 4th metacarpal pushed down and not visible
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What laboratory findings distinguish PHP from hypoparathyroidism?
cards.back: Both have low calcium and high phosphate, BUT PHP has elevated PTH (not low) - the key difference
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

cards.front: What is the Ellsworth-Howard test?
cards.back: Measures urinary cAMP and phosphate to differentiate hypoparathyroidism from PHP and its subtypes
cards.source: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007
cards.sourceFile: Parathyroid Disorders - CMED4IIM1 - 2026_pages-001-007.yml

atoms.source: Diagnosis & Classification of Diabetes Mellitus - CMED4IIM1
atoms.description:
Clinical stages of glycemia: normal (<6.1 mmol/L), impaired glucose tolerance (6.1-11.1 mmol/L at 2h), 
diabetes (≥11.1 mmol/L at 2h or ≥7 fasting)
atoms.srs_reference: Card: Diagnostic criteria for diabetes using fasting plasma glucose

atoms.description:
Type 1 presents with rapid DKA; Type 2 with slow onset, often overweight; LADA hybrid phenotype 
with autoimmune features
atoms.srs_reference: Card: Compare age of onset and body composition between type 1 and type 2

atoms.description:
Type 1 diabetes acute crisis: rapidly progresses to diabetic ketoacidosis with acidosis, 
ketonuria, hyperglycemia
atoms.srs_reference: Card: Acute crisis presentation for untreated type 1 vs type 2 diabetes

atoms.description:
Type 2 diabetes acute crisis: hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state without significant ketosis, 
severe hyperglycemia, dehydration

Hypoglycemia: adrenergic symptoms (tremor, palpitations, sweating, anxiety) and 
neuroglycopenic (confusion, headache, coma)

TSH suppressed (first), T4 elevated, T3 elevated; Graves disease with anti-TSH receptor antibody 
and eye signs
atoms.srs_reference: Card: Thyroid hormone measurement pattern in thyrotoxicosis

atoms.description:
Primary hypothyroidism: TSH elevated, T4 low; Secondary: TSH low, T4 low; 
Central: TSH low with inadequate T4 response
atoms.srs_reference: Card: Diagnosis of primary vs secondary hypothyroidism

atoms.source: Disorders of the Pituitary Gland - CMEDIIM1
atoms.description:
Isolated hypopituitarism: single hormone deficiency (ACTH, TSH, gonadotropins, GH); 
Pan-hypopituitarism: multiple deficiencies
atoms.srs_reference: Card: Difference between isolated and pan-hypopituitarism

atoms.description:
Excess GH and IGF-1 causing coarse features, prognathism, organomegaly, hypertension, glucose intolerance; 
fails to suppress on OGTT
atoms.srs_reference: Card: Gold standard provocative test for acromegaly - OGTT

atoms.description:
Prolactin >4× upper limit normal with galactorrhea, amenorrhea, infertility, decreased libido; 
MRI identifies macroadenoma

Addison disease: elevated ACTH, low cortisol, hyperpigmentation, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, 
hypoglycemia, weight loss
atoms.srs_reference: Card: Difference between primary and secondary adrenal insufficiency in ACTH levels

atoms.description:
Pituitary problem: low ACTH, low cortisol, NO hyperpigmentation, mild hyponatremia; 
distinguished from primary by low ACTH
atoms.srs_reference: Card: ACTH levels in primary vs secondary adrenal insufficiency

atoms.description:
Medical emergency: IV saline with high-dose hydrocortisone 100mg stat then 6-hourly, 
empiric antibiotics if infection suspected
atoms.srs_reference: Card: Treatment of an Addisonian crisis

atoms.description:
Excess cortisol: truncal obesity with thin limbs, moon facies, purple striae, myopathy, hypertension, 
dysglycemia, hypokalemia
atoms.srs_reference: Card: Low-dose dexamethasone suppression test for Cushing diagnosis

atoms.description:
Elevated PTH with elevated calcium, low phosphate; nephrolithiasis, nephrocalcinosis, bone disease, 
GI symptoms
atoms.srs_reference: Card: Key laboratory findings in primary hyperparathyroidism

atoms.description:
Compensatory PTH elevation from chronic renal disease, malabsorption, or rickets; 
low calcium triggers PTH rise

Malignancy with PTH-related protein: high calcium with LOW or ABSENT PTH 
(distinguishes from primary hyperparathyroidism)
atoms.srs_reference: Card: Ectopic hyperparathyroidism from PTH-rP secretion

atoms.description:
Calcium ≥3.5-3.7 mmol/L: shock-like state, oliguria, dehydration, pyrexia, coma; 
medical emergency requiring IV hydration
atoms.srs_reference: Card: Acute hypercalcemia as medical emergency

scenarios.name: Fasting Hyperglycemia
scenarios.description:
Patient presents with fasting glucose elevated, requires differentiation between 
impaired fasting glucose vs diabetes
scenarios.clinical_context:
52-year-old man with family history of diabetes presents for routine blood work.
Fasting glucose elevated on two separate occasions.
scenarios.expected_interpretation:
IFG (6.1-7 mmol/L) + IGT (2h 7.8-11.1 mmol/L) pattern suggests type 2 diabetes risk; 
needs confirmation on repeat testing; lifestyle modification recommended

scenarios.name: DKA Presentation
scenarios.description: Young patient with rapid-onset diabetes and metabolic acidosis
scenarios.clinical_context:
24-year-old woman presenting with 2 weeks of polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, 
and 1 day of nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, and drowsiness
scenarios.expected_interpretation:
DKA with severe hyperglycemia, anion gap metabolic acidosis (AG 18), high ketones; 
Type 1 diabetes new onset; requires urgent IV fluids, insulin, electrolyte monitoring
scenarios.recommended_action: ICU admission, IV fluids, insulin infusion, electrolyte replacement

scenarios.name: HHS - Hyperosmolar Hyperglycemic State
scenarios.description: Elderly patient with severe hyperglycemia without ketosis
scenarios.clinical_context:
78-year-old man with type 2 diabetes found confused at home after 3 days of 
polyuria and no fluid intake due to infection. Severely dehydrated.
scenarios.expected_interpretation:
HHS with severe hyperglycemia (55 mmol/L), hyperosmolarity (365), mild metabolic acidosis 
but NO ketonuria; Type 2 pattern; requires aggressive IV hydration and cautious insulin
scenarios.recommended_action: ICU admission, careful rehydration, slower insulin correction

scenarios.name: Hypoglycemia - Neuroglycopenic
scenarios.description: Diabetic patient on insulin with confusion and altered consciousness
scenarios.clinical_context:
45-year-old woman on insulin therapy presents with confusion, unable to talk coherently, 
tremor, and sweating; brought to ED by family
scenarios.expected_interpretation:
Severe hypoglycemia with neuroglycopenic symptoms (confusion, headache); 
adrenergic signs (tremor, sweating); requires urgent oral dextrose if conscious or IV if unable to swallow
scenarios.recommended_action: Immediate dextrose administration (15g oral or 25g IV), glucagon if severe

scenarios.name: Graves Disease
scenarios.description: Young woman with weight loss, tremor, and thyroid function showing suppressed TSH
scenarios.clinical_context:
35-year-old woman with 3-month history of progressive weight loss, anxiety, tremor, 
palpitations, heat intolerance. On examination: tachycardia, tremor, and eye protrusion noted.
scenarios.recommended_action: Anti-thyroid drugs (carbimazole), beta-blocker for symptoms, eye monitoring

scenarios.name: Primary Hypothyroidism
scenarios.description: Middle-aged patient with fatigue and cold intolerance
scenarios.clinical_context:
48-year-old woman with 6-month history of fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, 
dry skin, constipation. Has history of thyroid problems in family.
scenarios.recommended_action: Levothyroxine replacement, recheck TSH in 6-8 weeks

scenarios.name: Central Hypothyroidism
scenarios.description: Patient with low T4 and inadequately low TSH suggesting pituitary insufficiency
scenarios.clinical_context:
62-year-old man with history of pituitary adenoma surgery presenting with fatigue, 
cold intolerance. Labs show low T4 with TSH that should be high but is normal-low.
scenarios.recommended_action: MRI pituitary, assess all pituitary axes, levothyroxine + cortisol replacement

scenarios.name: Selective GH Deficiency
scenarios.description: Patient with isolated growth hormone deficiency
scenarios.clinical_context:
34-year-old man with history of childhood growth hormone deficiency, now with 
fatigue and metabolic abnormalities. Other pituitary hormones normal.
scenarios.recommended_action: GH stimulation testing, consider GH replacement if confirmed

scenarios.name: Acromegaly - GH Excess
scenarios.description: Patient with progressive enlargement and metabolic abnormalities
scenarios.clinical_context:
52-year-old man with progressive coarsening of facial features, hand enlargement, 
joint pain, hypertension. Wife notes increased sweating and snoring. Headaches present.
scenarios.recommended_action: Transphenoidal surgery, radiotherapy if surgical failure, somatostatin analogs

scenarios.name: Prolactinoma
scenarios.clinical_context:
32-year-old woman with 8 months of amenorrhea and spontaneous galactorrhea. 
Headaches and some vision problems noted. MRI shows pituitary mass.
scenarios.recommended_action: Dopamine agonist (cabergoline), visual field monitoring, MRI follow-up

scenarios.name: Primary Adrenal Insufficiency - Addison
scenarios.description: Patient with weight loss, hyperpigmentation, and electrolyte abnormalities
scenarios.clinical_context:
48-year-old woman with 3-month history of progressive weight loss, malaise, anorexia. 
On exam: hyperpigmentation over knuckles and creases, postural hypotension noted. 
Labs show hyponatremia and hyperkalemia.
scenarios.expected_interpretation:
Primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison) with low cortisol (95, <180 nmol/L at 0900), 
MARKEDLY elevated ACTH (185, >>50) - the compensatory response; hyponatremia (128) from 
aldosterone deficiency; hyperkalemia (5.8) from mineralocorticoid loss; hyperpigmentation 
from ACTH stimulation of melanocytes; Synacthen test shows no response (destroyed adrenal)
scenarios.recommended_action:
Glucocorticoid replacement (hydrocortisone 10mg am, 5mg lunch/evening) + 
mineralocorticoid (fludrocortisone 0.05-0.1mg daily)

scenarios.name: Secondary Adrenal Insufficiency
scenarios.description: Patient with pituitary disease and cortisol deficiency
scenarios.clinical_context:
55-year-old man post-pituitary surgery for adenoma now with fatigue, malaise, 
but no hyperpigmentation. Labs show low cortisol and inappropriately low ACTH.
scenarios.expected_interpretation:
Secondary adrenal insufficiency (pituitary dysfunction) with low cortisol (85) but LOW ACTH (8) 
- the key distinguishing feature from primary; NO hyperpigmentation (ACTH is low so cannot stimulate melanocytes); 
mild hyponatremia (132) only from cortisol loss (no aldosterone deficiency as adrenal aldosterone 
is primarily controlled by renin-angiotensin); normal potassium

scenarios.name: Addisonian Crisis
scenarios.description: Medical emergency with shock and severe electrolyte derangement
scenarios.clinical_context:
42-year-old woman with known Addison disease admitted with shock, confusion, 
severe dehydration following illness. On examination: profound hypotension (SBP 82), 
severely ill appearance, confusion.
scenarios.expected_interpretation:
Addisonian crisis - MEDICAL EMERGENCY: acute decompensation with shock (SBP 82), 
severe hyponatremia (118), severe hyperkalemia (6.5), altered consciousness; 
Cortisol critically low (65); ACTH markedly elevated (250); precipitating infection 
likely given systemic illness

scenarios.name: Cushing Syndrome
scenarios.description: Patient with truncal obesity, muscle wasting, and hypertension
scenarios.clinical_context:
38-year-old woman with progressive weight gain (truncal), muscle weakness, 
hypertension (BP 165/95), and skin changes (purple striae, easy bruising). 
Mood changes and insomnia noted.
differential_diagnosis: Ectopic ACTH (carcinoid, small cell lung cancer)
scenarios.recommended_action:
High-dose dexamethasone test (8mg) for localization; IPSS if pituitary suspected; 
Imaging (MRI pituitary, CT chest/abdomen); transphenoidal surgery if pituitary

scenarios.name: Primary Hyperparathyroidism
scenarios.description: Patient with elevated calcium and inappropriate PTH elevation
scenarios.clinical_context:
58-year-old woman with history of kidney stones presenting with hypercalcemia, 
mild renal impairment, and bone pain. Labs show elevated calcium with inappropriately 
elevated PTH.
scenarios.expected_interpretation:
Primary hyperparathyroidism with elevated calcium (3.2) and inappropriately elevated PTH (185) - 
this combination is pathognomonic for primary hyperparathyroidism; low phosphate (0.65) typical 
(PTH increases urinary phosphate excretion); elevated alkaline phosphatase (125) suggests bone turnover; 
History of nephrolithiasis typical; Imaging would show bone changes (osteitis fibrosa cystica, 
subperiosteal reabsorption)
scenarios.recommended_action: Parathyroidectomy (definitive), or medical surveillance if asymptomatic

scenarios.name: Secondary Hyperparathyroidism - CKD
scenarios.description: Patient with chronic kidney disease and compensatory PTH elevation
scenarios.clinical_context:
68-year-old man with stage 3b CKD (eGFR 18) on dialysis presenting with bone pain 
and muscle cramps. Labs show low calcium and high phosphate with markedly elevated PTH.
scenarios.expected_interpretation:
Secondary hyperparathyroidism from chronic kidney disease: PTH markedly elevated (310) 
in RESPONSE TO low calcium (2.0) and phosphate retention (2.1, high); Kidney disease 
(Cr 380, eGFR 18) causes: loss of 1,25-vitamin D production → low calcium → secondary PTH rise; 
retention of phosphate also stimulates PTH; This is compensatory hyperparathyroidism, 
NOT primary PTH excess (PTH is appropriately high given the stimulus)
scenarios.recommended_action:
Phosphate binders, active vitamin D or calcineuretics, PTH-lowering agents (cinacalcet) 
if PTH >300

scenarios.name: Hypercalcemia - Ectopic PTHrP
scenarios.description: Patient with malignancy and hypercalcemia but SUPPRESSED PTH
scenarios.clinical_context:
64-year-old man with newly diagnosed lung carcinoma (squamous cell) presenting with 
hypercalcemia, polyuria, constipation. PTH measured and found to be LOW despite high calcium.
scenarios.expected_interpretation:
Humoral hypercalcemia of malignancy (ectopic PTH-rP secretion): HIGH calcium (3.5) 
with LOW/SUPPRESSED PTH (18, <10 is low) and ELEVATED PTH-rP (8.5); This pattern 
is OPPOSITE to primary hyperparathyroidism (where PTH is high); PTH-rP mimics PTH 
effects causing calcium rise and phosphate decrease; commonly seen in squamous cell lung 
cancer, breast cancer, renal cancer
scenarios.recommended_action:
Treat underlying malignancy; aggressive hydration; bisphosphonates or denosumab 
for calcium control

scenarios.name: Acute Hypercalcemia Emergency
scenarios.description: Medical emergency with severe symptomatic hypercalcemia
scenarios.clinical_context:
71-year-old man with primary hyperparathyroidism presenting with severe confusion, 
coma-like state, severe dehydration, and oliguria. Found unresponsive at home.
scenarios.expected_interpretation:
Acute hypercalcemia emergency (MEDICAL EMERGENCY): Calcium 3.8 mmol/L (normal <2.6), 
comatose consciousness, severe oliguria (200 mL/day), elevated osmolality (365) from 
severe dehydration; Elevated creatinine (285) from prerenal azotemia; Hypercalcemia 
causes nephrogenic DI → polyuria → severe dehydration if intake not maintained; 
Hypercalcemia directly toxic to CNS causing altered consciousness
